hints.profundis: 'profundis' is a noun/adjective in the ablative plural meaning 'the depths'
hints.te: 'te' is a personal pronoun in the accusative/ablative meaning 'you'

hints.to close: 'to close' is 'claudere' — 'clamavi' comes from 'clamare' (to cry out)
hints.to claim: 'to claim' is a later/English derivative — 'clamare' means 'to cry out/shout'
hints.to call: 'to call' is 'vocare' — 'clamavi' comes from 'clamare' (to cry out)

hints.Present: Present of 'clamare' 1st person would be 'clamo' — 'clamavi' has the perfect -avi ending
hints.Imperfect: Imperfect of 'clamare' 1st person would be 'clamabam' — 'clamavi' has the perfect -avi ending
hints.Pluperfect: Pluperfect of 'clamare' 1st person would be 'clamaveram' — 'clamavi' lacks the -era- marker

hints.claudo, claudere, clausi, clausum: These are the principal parts of 'claudere' (to close/shut)
hints.voco, vocare, vocavi, vocatum: These are the principal parts of 'vocare' (to call)
hints.credo, credere, credidi, creditum: These are the principal parts of 'credere' (to believe)

hints.Nominative: Nominative plural of 'profundum' would be 'profunda' — 'profundis' is dative or ablative plural
hints.Genitive: Genitive plural of 'profundum' would be 'profundorum' — 'profundis' is ablative after 'de'
hints.Dative: While 'profundis' could be dative plural, after the preposition 'de' it must be ablative

hints.1st declension: 1st declension nouns have genitive in -ae — 'profundi' ends in -i
hints.3rd declension: 3rd declension nouns have genitive in -is — 'profundi' ends in -i
hints.4th declension: 4th declension nouns have genitive in -ūs — 'profundi' ends in -i

hints.Nominative: Nominative of 'tu' is 'tu' — 'te' is the accusative or ablative form
hints.Genitive: Genitive of 'tu' would be 'tui' — 'te' is accusative after the preposition 'ad'
hints.Ablative: While 'te' can be ablative, after 'ad' (which takes accusative) it is accusative

hints.Nominative case: No preposition governs the nominative — 'ad' takes the accusative
hints.Genitive case: Prepositions like 'causa' take genitive — 'ad' takes the accusative
hints.Ablative case: Prepositions like 'de', 'ex', 'ab' take ablative — 'ad' takes the accusative

translation: Out of the depths I have cried to you
